What Drives Mobile Data Consumption? Traffic Composition, Regional Divergence, and 5G Coverage, 2018–2025

Author

JSC370 Final Project, Nanxin Li

Introduction

Mobile data traffic has grown from roughly 16 EB/month in early 2018 to more than 200 EB/month by late 2025, driven by video streaming, social media, and the global expansion of smartphone connectivity. INvestigating what drives this growth, and where the inbalance remain, matters for network planning, spectrum policy, and digital equity.

This project uses data from the Ericsson Mobility Report / Data portal, the telecommunications industry’s most cited source for mobile network traffic measurements and forecasts. All variables are directly reported in Ericsson’s published Excel exports.

4 research questions are included in this report. First, how has global mobile traffic composition changed from 2018 to 2025, and which categorie are driving growth? Second, how large are inter-regional differences in per-subscription data consumption, and is the gap widening or narrowing? Third, what is the empirical relationship between global 5G population coverage and per-subscription consumption? Fourth, do regions with low data useage per subcription grow faster than regions with high data useage per subsciption?

Methods

Data Sources

All data were aggregated from the Ericsson Mobility Report, accessed through the Ericsson Mobility Visualizer. Four datasets are used:

  • Mobile data traffic by application category (global, 2018–2025): monthly mobile traffic in EB/month broken down into seven categories eg. Video, Social Networking, Web Browsing, Audio, File Sharing, Software Update, and Other.
  • Mobile data traffic per device per month (regional, 2018–2025): average monthly data consumption per smartphone subscription by region.
  • Population coverage (global, 2018–2025): share of world population covered by 5G networks.
  • Mobile network traffic (global, quarterly, 2018–2025): total mobile data volume (EB/month) and year-over-year growth rate.

The dataset used here provide nine regional data after excluding overlapping regions. They are C & E Europe, India & South Asia, Latin America, Middle East & Africa, North America, North East Asia, SE Asia & Oceania, Sub-Saharan Africa, and Western Europe.

Analytical Approach

Research Questions 1, 2, 3 uses descriptive analysis and correlation. Research Question 4 uses two complementary statistical models.

Model A — Panel OLS with region fixed effects: The target variable is log(GB/month) per subscription across different regions. The model include regional fixed effects, absorbing all time-invariant regional features such as income level, infrastructure quality, and device ecosystem maturity, and a continuous year variable. This isolates the within-region time trend in data useage while controlling structural differences between regions.

\[ \log(\text{GB/month})_{it} = \alpha_i + \beta \cdot \text{year}_t + \varepsilon_{it} \]

\(i\) represents different regions. eg. \(i = 1\) represents North America. \(t\) represents different years. eg. \(t = 0\) represents Year 2018. \(\alpha_i\) is the baseline which differs across different regions. e.g. \(\alpha_1\) represents the specefic basedline for \(\log(\text{GB/month})\) in North America in 2018. \(\beta\) is the fixed effect for increasement in \(\log(\text{GB/month})\) in each year, keeps the same for all 9 regions. \(\varepsilon_{it}\) is the variance for each prediction. \(Var(\varepsilon_{it})\) keeps the same for all i and t.

Model B — Cross-sectional OLS (catch-up test): Model calculates each region’s 2018–2025 compound annual growth rate (CAGR) on its 2018 baseline consumption level. A negative coefficient on the baseline indicates the regions has low data consumption rate is catching up, with low-baseline regions growing faster in term of percentage. An important insight is that percentage catch-up does not necessarily reduce the gap in absolute amount; that question is assessed separately by comparing absolute GB/month differences in 2018 and 2025.

\[ \text{CAGR}_i = \alpha + \beta \cdot \text{GB}_{2018,i} + \varepsilon_i \]

\(\text{CAGR}_i = \sqrt[7]{\frac{\text{GB}_{2025,i}}{\text{GB}_{2018,i}}} - 1\) \(i\) represents different regions. eg. \(i = 1\) represents North America. \(\alpha\) is the shared baseline. e.g. \(\alpha\) represents the basedline \(CAGR\) for \(\log(\text{GB/month})\) for regions with 0 GB/month data useage in 2018. \(\beta\) is the fixed effect for increasement in \(CAGR\) for each addtional \(GB/month\) used in 2018, keeps the same for all 9 regions. \(\varepsilon_{i}\) is the variance for each prediction. \(Var(\varepsilon_{i})\) keeps the same for all i.

The two OLS models above has advantages in interpretability. The coefficients owns direct economic meaning, making them suitable for panel data with small sample sizes. But they force assumptions of linear relationships, such as assuming uniform effort of the impact of 5G coverage on per capita data traffic, but in reality, the diffusion effect tends to be non-linear. GAM automatically fits non-linear relationships using spline functions, without specifying the function form in advance, making it flexible in capturing the curve effects of technology diffusion. But with extremely small samples, the degrees of freedom are insufficient to prevent overfitting, resulting in unreliable outputs, and the coefficients lacks intuitive causal interpretation. Therefore, the data scale of this project determines that OLS is a more reasonable choice.

Results

RQ1: Traffic Composition

Figure 1 shows that video streaming keeps dominaing global mobile traffic throughout 2018 to 2025, and it’s continued to grow in absolute volume, from 13.4 EB/month in 2018 to 108.4 EB/month in 2025. Its percentage share also increased, rising from 59.9% to 75.9% of total traffic over the period.

Table 1 shows monthly total / annual growth rates by category for 2018–2025. Video usage grews fastest at 34.8% per year, followed by Social Networking at 23.2% and Other at 21.9%. Web Browsing showed the slowest growth at 11.4%, consistent with the shift from web-page based apps to mobile apps. Software Update traffic also grew quickly, reflecting the rising size and preference of operating system and application updates on connected devices over 5G network.

These growth patterns implies that network capacity planning must remain video-centric while still keep remaining capacity for social and cloud-based categories. The consistancy of video’s dominance means long-run traffic forecasting are highly sensitive to assumptions about video resolution and autoplay behavior.

RQ2: Regional Divergence

Figure 2 shows that India & South Asia leads all regions in terms of per-subscription consumption by 2025 at 36.0 GB/month, a result consistent with aggressive low-cost unlimited data plans that made India one of the world’s highest per-subscriber traffic markets despite its lower income level. Sub-Saharan Africa remains the lowest at 5.3 GB/month in 2025.

The absolute gap between the highest and lowest data consuming regions raised from 7.3 GB/month in 2018 to 30.7 GB/month in 2025. This widening occurred disregarding positive growth in every region, showing a digital equity tension, as percentage growth in lagging regions is not sufficient enough to close absolute gaps when leading regions are also growing rapidly.

RQ3: 5G Coverage and World-Average Consumption

Figure 3 plots global 5G population coverage against world-average per-subscription consumption for 2018–2025. The Pearson correlation is 0.994 accompanied by p = 0.0000, indicating a very strong positive association. In the OLS line, each additional percentage point of 5G coverage corresponds to approximately 0.249 GB/month in per-subscription consumption.

Two limitations are worth noticing. First, year(time) is a strong confounding variable, as both 5G coverage and per-subscription consumption increase monotonically over time, so the correlation partially reflects shared temporal trends rather than a direct ‘5G effect’. Second, with only eight years of observations, the analysis has limited statistical power for structural interpretation. The finding should therefore be treated as a descriptive insight rather than a causal evidence.

Even with those limitations, the pattern is consistent with the industry expectation that 5G enables higher-bandwidth applications, especially higher-resolution video and FWA, that raise per-subscription consumption beyond what speed improvements alone would contribute.

RQ4: Modeling Catch-Up and Divergence

Table 2: Panel OLS key coefficient and variance components

Term Coef Std. Err. t p-value
year_centered 0.226 0.008 29.172 0.000
Var(alpha_i) 0.342
Var(epsilon) 0.023

Panel model statistics: n = 72, R-squared = 0.967, adjusted R-squared = 0.962. Var(alpha_i) is computed from the estimated region fixed-effect intercepts; Var(epsilon) is the residual variance.

Table 3: Cross-sectional OLS coefficients

Term Coef Std. Err. t p-value
Intercept 30.441 2.214 13.747 0.000
GB_2018 -1.198 0.426 -2.814 0.026

Catch-up model statistics: n = 9, R-squared = 0.531.

Model A: Panel OLS with Region Fixed Effects

The panel OLS model predicts log(GB/month) on year, centered at 2018, and region fixed effects across all 72 region-year observations. The year coefficient is 0.226 (p < 0.001), implying that within each region, per-subscription consumption grew at approximately 25.3% per year on average after isolating time-invariant regional differences. The model achieves R² = 0.967, indicating that year and region fixed effects together explain 96.7% of the variance in log(GB/month), dispite the differences in starting amount persists. ICC = 0.9369, indicating significant inter-regional differences.

The regional fixed effects absorbs persistent differences in income level, infrastructure quality, regulatory environment, pricing structure, and device ecosystem maturity etc. The year coefficient therefore captures the common temporal growth trend in mobile data consumption that appears across all regions.

Model B: Cross-Sectional Catch-Up Test

The cross-sectional OLS model regresses across each region’s 2018–2025 CAGR on its 2018 baseline consumption. The slope coefficient is -1.198 with p = 0.026, indicating that a one GB/month higher in data consumption in 2018 baseline is associated with a 1.20 percentage point lower annual growth rate over 2018–2025. This supports a catch-up hypothesis at relatively trustworthy significance levels.

However, percentage catch-up in growth rates does not imply convergence in absolute mobile data consumptions. The absolute gap between the highest- and lowest-consuming regions widens from 7.3 to 30.7 GB per month over 2018 to 2025, even as lower-baseline regions grew faster in terms of percentage. For digital equity, the raising gap in absolute connectivity capacity is a more critical insight.

Interactive Visualizations

The project includes five interactive visualizations on website and in viz.html. They allows hover inspection, toggles, dropdown filtering, animation, 2 axis exploration for the quarterly traffic series and a global year-increase visualization.

Conclusions and Summary

Traffic composition: Video is the structurally dominant category of global mobile traffic, contributing the most EB/month throughout 2018–2025. In Ericsson’s report, it is also the fastest-growing category at 34.8% per year, while Social Networking is the fastest-growing non-video category at 23.2% per year. Web Browsing grows slowest, back-proofing the shift toward app-based and streaming-heavy mobile use.

Regional divergence: Per-subscription consumption grows significantly across all regions, but the absolute gap between the highest and lowest mobile data consuming regions widened from 7.3 to 30.7 GB/month. India & South Asia leads by 2025 at 36.0 GB/month, while Sub-Saharan Africa remains the lowest at 5.3 GB/month.

5G and consumption: The Pearson correlation between global 5G coverage and world-average per-subscription consumption is 0.994, accompanied by a low p, with year contributing as a major confounding factor. The association is strong but should not be interpreted isolated from other time-series data.

Catch-up dynamics: Panel OLS shows a within-region annual growth rate of about 25.3% per year after controlling for region fixed effects. The cross-sectional catch-up test finds a negative slope (-1.198, p = 0.026) on baseline data consumption, indicating a percentage catch-up. But catch-up in growth rates has not prevented widening in absolute consumption gaps, which is a concern for digital equity.

The main limitation is data sparsity. With 9 regions and 8 years, statistical power remains limited. Country-level rollout and traffic data would enable stronger causal inference. Future work should also incorporate 5G-specific subscription counts and traffic volumes, which ITU has begun to publish, to measure the 5G effect on consumption more directly than coverage alone allows.
